pattern: Sort elements by frequency descending and greedily pick the highest-frequency values first, stopping as soon as the running removal count reaches at least half the array size.
hints: Count how many times each distinct value appears. A value with higher frequency removes more elements when chosen, so it is the better pick.
Sort the distinct values by their frequency in descending order. Add them to your set one by one, accumulating how many total elements you have removed.
Stop the moment the cumulative removal count is at least half the original array length. The number of values picked so far is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: one value dominates more than half the array
mixed frequencies — need two values to cover half
all distinct — every element appears once, need exactly n/2 picks
minimum array length of two, both same
minimum array length of two, both different
two values each at half — one pick is enough
frequencies tied across many values — tie-breaking doesn't affect count but stress correctness

stress.description: large array with skewed frequency distribution
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size)
    if n % 2 != 0:
        n += 1
    values = list(range(1, 101))
    weights = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in values]
    total = sum(weights)
    arr = []
    for v, w in zip(values, weights):
        count = max(1, round(w / total * n))
        arr.extend([v] * count)
    arr = arr[:n]
    while len(arr) < n:
        arr.append(rng.choice(values))
    if len(arr) % 2 != 0:
        arr.append(rng.choice(values))
    rng.shuffle(arr)
    return [arr]
